---
title: "Statistics Mentorship Program"
output: html_document
date: "2026-04-16"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```


## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

## Load library/packages - need to run this every time you open RStudio

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)
```

## Reading file for your project

```{r}
co2 <-read.csv("/Users/Nyx/Onedrive/Desktop/R/Datasets/Raw/owid-co2-data.csv")

energy <-read.csv("/Users/Nyx/Onedrive/Desktop/R/Datasets/Raw/owid-energy-data.csv")

df_joined <-read.csv("/Users/Nyx/Onedrive/Desktop/R/Datasets/Raw/df_joined.csv")

df_joinedW <-read.csv("/Users/Nyx/Onedrive/Desktop/R/Datasets/Working/df_joinedW.csv")
```

#Saving your working dataset 

```{r}
write.csv(df_joinedW, file = "/Users/Nyx/Onedrive/Desktop/R/Datasets/working/df_joinedW.csv")
```

## Join-Clean Dataset

```{r}
energy_select <- energy |>
  select(
    country, year,
    renewables_share_energy,
    fossil_share_energy,
    renewables_share_elec,
    renewables_consumption,
    low_carbon_share_energy
  )

#I need to derive gdp_per_capta = gdp / population after the join
co2_select <- co2 |>
  select(
    country, year, iso_code,
    co2_per_capita,
    co2,
    gdp,
    population
  )

df_joined <- co2_select |>
  left_join(energy_select, by = c("country", "year"))

cat("Joined dataset: ", nrow(df_joined), "rows", ncol(df_joined), "columns\n")
```

## What do we have?

```{r}
dim(df_joined)       # rows x columns
colnames(df_joined)  # variable names
glimpse(df_joined)   # column types -- are they correct?
head(df_joined)      # first 6 rows
```

## Are there missing values?

```{r check-missing}
# summary() shows NA counts for numeric columns
summary(df_joined)

# Count NAs in every column at once
colSums(is.na(df_joined))
```

## Do the categories look clean?

```{r check-categories}
table(df_joined$co2_per_capita)
```

## Filter 2020-2022 & countries with limited data

```{r}
df_joinedW <- df_joined |>
  filter(year >= 2020 & year <= 2022,
         !country %in% c("Christmas Island", "Least developed countries (Jones et al.)","Kuwaiti Oil Fires (GCP)", "Kuwaiti Oil Fires", "OECD (Jones et al.)", "Ryukyu Islands", "Ryukyu Islands (GCP)" )) #monaco, san marino, vatican only have population data
```

## The basic problem (NA)

```{r na-case1-correct}
# CORRECT -- na.rm = TRUE, and always report how many were excluded
df_joinedW %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  summarise(
    n_total         = n(),
    n_missing       = sum(is.na(co2_per_capita)),
    percent_missing = round(n_missing / n_total * 100, 1),
    mean_co2        = mean(co2_per_capita, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_co2          = sd(co2_per_capita,   na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```

## Dropping NAs carelessly creates a false result

Imagine the 8 missing BDNF values all come from the AD patients
whose samples failed quality control. If we filter out NA rows, we silently
remove those patients. The remaining AD group now contains only milder cases,
and any comparison to controls will *underestimate* the true difference.

```{r na-case2}
set.seed(99)
# Simulate bias: remove lowest CO2 values from one country
df_biased <- df_joinedW
target_rows <- which(df_biased$country == "Canada" & !is.na(df_biased$co2_per_capita))
# pick lowest values
lowest_vals <- target_rows[order(df_biased$co2_per_capita[target_rows])][1:1]
# turn them into NA
df_biased$co2_per_capita[lowest_vals] <- NA

# biased: drop NA rows completely
biased_result <- df_biased %>%
  filter(!is.na(co2_per_capita)) %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  summarise(
    n_analysed = n(),
    mean_co2   = round(mean(co2_per_capita), 3)
  )


# unbiased: keep data, just ignore NA in calculation
unbiased_result <- df_joinedW %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  summarise(
    n_analysed = sum(!is.na(co2_per_capita)),
    mean_co2   = round(mean(co2_per_capita, na.rm = TRUE), 3)
  )

cat("--- Biased (rows silently removed) ---\n")
print(biased_result)
cat("\n--- Unbiased (all rows accounted for) ---\n")
print(unbiased_result)
```

## Percent missing from each column

```{r drop-column-check}
# Check percentage missing per column
data.frame(
  column      = colnames(df_joinedW),
  n_missing   = colSums(is.na(df_joinedW)),
  pct_missing = round(colSums(is.na(df_joinedW)) / nrow(df_joinedW) * 100, 1)
)
```

**Practical guideline:**

| % missing in a column | Recommended action |
|------------------------|-------------------|
| Less than 10%  | Use `na.rm = TRUE`; report the count |
| 10% to 40%     | Be cautious; check if missingness is random; report it |
| More than 40%  | Consider dropping the column entirely |
